summary: The post responds to a debate between Chris Dixon and Ev Williams about whether the internet is destined to become dominated by proprietary, centralized services over open protocols. Dixon warned we're on the wrong side of history, while Williams argued that media industries naturally settle into closed systems. Asparouhova offers several reasons for optimism: empowered content creators, low barriers to building products, the rise of patronage models, and blockchain technology. She argues the internet is fundamentally different from radio or television because it is not merely a distribution channel but an amalgamation of many channels. Her conclusion is that while individual channels may cycle through innovation and centralization, the internet's rapid pace means empires are built and broken overnight.
key_points: Content creators with enough critical mass can always take their audience elsewhere, creating tension with centralized platforms
The low barrier to building products gives more people agency over shaping the internet
Patronage models and blockchain technology could shift power away from centralized platforms
The internet differs fundamentally from radio or TV because it is an amalgamation of many distribution channels, not a single one
Individual channels may centralize, but the rapid cycle of innovation on the internet means the pendulum keeps swinging
money_quotes: In the end, with enough critical mass, mini moguls can always take their audience elsewhere.
The easier it is for anybody to shape the internet, the more agency people will want over their ideas.
Unlike radio or television, the internet is more than just media. The internet is how we work, think, and communicate.
Radio or television ARE distribution channels; the internet is an amalgamation of many of those channels - many radios or televisions - and new ones are being created all the time.
Maybe each channel is doomed to go through the cycle of innovation -> centralization, but those cycles repeat themselves rapidly on the internet.
Empires are built and broken overnight, and the pendulum will continue to swing back and forth.
key_insight: The internet is not a single distribution channel like radio or TV but an amalgamation of many channels, so while individual platforms may centralize, the rapid creation of new channels ensures the pendulum between openness and centralization keeps swinging.
